does: This paper introduces Weighted Preference Optimization (WPO), a method that addresses the distributional gap problem in off-policy preference optimization for RLHF. The core contribution is a reweighting strategy that adapts off-policy preference data collected from other models to better approximate on-policy data by reweighting preference pairs according to their probability under the current policy being trained. The method simulates on-policy learning dynamics without requiring additional data collection, making it computationally efficient. The authors validate WPO on instruction-following benchmarks including Alpaca Eval 2 and MT-bench, demonstrating improvements over standard preference optimization approaches.
connects: This work builds directly on the recent wave of off-policy preference optimization methods like DPO and IPO that replaced traditional RLHF due to their scalability and lower cost. These methods use preference data from fixed reference models rather than interactively collecting data from the evolving policy, but suffer from distributional shift when the target policy diverges significantly from the data collection policy. WPO extends this line by addressing a fundamental problem identified in prior work: the gap between off-policy and on-policy learning. The reweighting approach draws conceptually from importance sampling and sample reweighting techniques used in off-policy reinforcement learning, adapting these principles to the preference optimization setting.
enables: This work opens directions for more sophisticated distribution-matching techniques in preference learning. The reweighting framework could inspire importance-weighted variants of other preference optimization methods, potentially enabling better control over the exploration-exploitation tradeoff during fine-tuning. The approach suggests pathways toward theoretically principled off-policy learning that recovers on-policy guarantees without retraining, which could scale preference optimization to even larger models and datasets. It also raises questions about optimal weighting schemes and when reweighting alone suffices versus when active data collection becomes necessary, setting up future work on hybrid on-policy and off-policy strategies for LLM alignment.
